text: Sentencing
Mitigation
Aggravation
Remorse
Custodial
Probation
Proportionate
Rehabilitate
Leniency
Amends
Gravity
Recidivism
The state should have offered treatment years earlier.
He could have fled, but he stayed and confessed.
He might have caused far greater harm.
The court ought to have shown more leniency.
The verdict is guilty. Counsel, before I pass sentence, what do you say in mitigation?
Your Honour, I do not dispute the gravity of this offence. But in mitigation, my client could have fled and instead he stayed and confessed.
He has shown genuine remorse, and he has already begun to make amends to the victim. The system should have offered him treatment years earlier.
The prosecution points to aggravation, a breach of trust. How do you answer that?
I concede it, Your Honour. But he might have caused far greater harm, and he chose to stop. A custodial term would not rehabilitate him; it would only deepen the harm.
I ask the court for leniency: a proportionate sentence, perhaps probation, that punishes without ending a young life.
I accept the gravity, counsel, but also the remorse. The court will consider a sentence below the maximum.

Once the verdict is in, your real work as a defender begins, because sentencing is where a human life is weighed. Never argue the facts again; the guilt is settled. Argue the person. Lay out the mitigating factors first: a clean record, a hard childhood, genuine remorse, a willingness to make amends. The defendant could have run, but he stayed and confessed. He might have blamed others, yet he took responsibility. Concede the aggravating factors honestly, because the judge already knows them, and hiding them costs you trust. Then ask for proportion. A sentence should fit the offence, not the anger of the moment. Remind the court that the goal is not only to punish, but to rehabilitate. The judge ought to leave a door open, and it is your job to show where that door is.

You are listening to the legal affairs review. At the sentencing hearing in the State versus Andrade, the courtroom heard two very different portraits of the same man. The prosecution stressed the aggravating circumstances: the breach of trust and the harm to the victim, arguing that the offence warranted the maximum term. The defence answered with mitigation. Counsel acknowledged the gravity, but argued that the defendant should have been offered treatment years earlier, and that he could have caused far greater harm yet chose to stop. Although the judge accepted that the crime was serious, she agreed that remorse and a clean record counted for something. The sentence handed down was custodial, but well below the maximum. Observers called it a balanced outcome. We will report on the appeal if one is filed.
